methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $25M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Biotech & Health. Range: $100M - $3.3B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 7.4x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Zeta Surgical

data.narrative:
Cellcolabs is a Series A biotech company founded by Mattias Bernow that has raised $25M+. The valuation analysis triangulates across a peer universe of 8 private and 3 public comparables in Biotech & Health, including Series B-stage companies such as Eikon Therapeutics, Neko Health, New Limit, Asimov, and Profluent.

Two valuation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis, using total raised as a proxy for current value and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement, implies a $50M valuation. Peer-median analysis across the 6 same-sector, same/adjacent-stage private comparables yields a median valuation of $805M, with a range spanning $100M to $3.3B. Public-comp anchoring cannot be applied due to absence of Cellcolabs revenue data, though the Biotech & Health sector median trades at 7.4x price-to-sales across 3 public tickers (RXRX, TEM, ONT.L).

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $42M (low) to $926M (high), with a median of $428M. The wide dispersion reflects material uncertainty regarding Cellcolabs' specific technology maturity, IP strength, and competitive positioning relative to the peer cohort. The peer-median method suggests substantially higher value than stage-progression alone, implying the market has priced meaningful optionality into Series B biotech valuations. Revenue trajectory and clinical/commercial validation milestones represent key de-risking factors that will narrow this range.
